nativeRange.habitat: Deep coral-rich reefs and reef drop-offs, typically found at depths of 15–55 m (up to 100 m)

diet.notes: In the wild this species has been documented nibbling on the tube feet of sea urchins and the tentacles of tubeworms, as well as consuming small invertebrates. In captivity it tends to accept meaty foods including mysis shrimp, marine fish flesh, crustacean flesh, and high-quality frozen carnivore preparations; frequent small feedings (at least three times daily) are generally advised.

imageCandidates.notes: NOAA Photo Library; 3613x2400. Sharp in-situ reef shot of full fish showing all diagnostic features (yellow pointed snout, yellow eye bar, orange-yellow upper body, dark oblique bar on rear/dorsal, yellow ventral and tail). Clean, no watermark, highest resolution.
Dan Schofield; 2048x1537. Vivid, sharp side profile with strong coloration and clear diagnostic dark diagonal bar; small '(c) Dan Schofield' watermark in lower-left corner is the only caveat.

summary: The Longnose Atlantic Butterflyfish (Prognathodes aculeatus) is a Caribbean reef species generally considered one of the more aquarium-adaptable members of its family. It tends to accept frozen foods more readily than many butterflyfish, though reef placement warrants caution due to its invertebrate-nipping tendencies.
careNotes: The Longnose Atlantic Butterflyfish is generally considered one of the hardier butterflyfish for aquarium keeping, and tends to accept frozen and prepared carnivore foods more readily than many of its relatives. It is best kept singly or as a mated pair; housing with other butterflyfish species has been known to cause aggression and stress. While generally peaceful with dissimilar tankmates, it has been reported to nip at LPS corals (large-polyp stony corals), gorgonians, tubeworms, feather dusters, and small snails, so placement in a reef aquarium warrants caution. A setup that provides ample live rock, caves, and open swimming space suits this species well; in the wild it naturally inhabits deeper reef zones at 15–55 m and tends to prefer subdued or moderate lighting in the aquarium. This species forms monogamous pair bonds in the wild and is sometimes available as mated pairs; established pairs should not be separated.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been rarely documented. In the wild, pairs have been observed depositing eggs on hard surfaces during the breeding season, with larvae being planktonic before settlement. Captive reproduction is generally considered impractical under normal aquarium conditions.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (confirmed max 10.0 cm), temperature range (22.9–28°C), native distribution, taxonomy (family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes), and behavior (solitary or in pairs, nibbles invertebrates).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (75 gal / ~284 L recommended, higher than LiveAquaria's 50 gal); adult size confirmed at 4 inches; care level listed as Intermediate.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely if ever achieved. Wild pairs spawn on hard substrates; larvae are pelagic.
